content.text: The Role of Change Champions in Claude Deployments

A change champion is an individual contributor or team lead within the business unit who voluntarily supports the adoption of a new Claude integration. Unlike the executive sponsor (who provides authority and budget) or the programme team (who build the system), champions work at peer level. They are the colleagues who answer questions in Slack, demonstrate the tool in team meetings, surface friction that users would not escalate formally, and make the new workflow feel normal.

Champions are not created by appointment. A manager who designates a reluctant team member as 'the AI champion' produces a nominal rather than a real one. The programme team's job is to identify people who are naturally positioned to play this role — and then recruit, equip, and sustain them deliberately.

At the right ratio (typically one champion per 8–12 users), a champion network can be the difference between a pilot that achieves 60% active usage and one that achieves 15%. The effect is strongest in large BU rollouts where the programme team cannot be present on the ground and in organisations where AI scepticism is high enough that formal communications are discounted.
Champion Selection Criteria

attrs.alt: Champion selection criteria: identity criteria (who they are) and role criteria (what they do)

content.text: Two types of criteria apply when selecting champions. Identity criteria describe the intrinsic characteristics that make someone an effective champion — these cannot be trained quickly. Role criteria describe the activities the champion will perform — these can be equipped and supported.
Identifying Candidates

Candidates surface through three mechanisms. The first is nomination: ask the BU sponsor and team leads to identify the people their teams go to for help with new tools. The second is observation: in the first few discovery workshop sessions, some participants will ask sharper questions, propose workarounds, or express genuine curiosity about the technology. These are self-identifying candidates. The third is self-selection: a brief call-to-action in the programme launch communication ('We are looking for pilot participants who want to help shape the tool') attracts willing candidates.

Do not rely solely on management nomination. Managers tend to nominate their most experienced or senior people — who may have the highest investment in the existing process and therefore the strongest resistance to changing it. The effective champion is often the person two levels below the team lead who everyone already asks for help.
Validation Conversation
Before recruiting a candidate, run a 20-minute one-on-one conversation to assess fit. Key questions:
How do you currently feel about AI tools in your work — have you tried any? What worked, what did not?
When something new is introduced in your team, what typically happens? Where does it get stuck?
If we gave you early access to this tool before the full team, would that be something you'd want? Why?
Are you comfortable being the first person on your team to use this and answer questions about it?
What would make you feel properly set up to play this role?

The answers reveal curiosity, process knowledge, and comfort with uncertainty. Candidates who frame AI adoption in terms of their own benefit ('I want to learn this') are better choices than those who frame it as a duty ('I'll do it if my manager wants me to'). Red flags: significant resistance to the existing change, prior bad experience with poorly rolled-out tools that created resentment, or time constraints that would make 2h/week impossible.
The Champion Engagement Lifecycle

attrs.alt: Champion engagement lifecycle showing five phases: Identify, Recruit, Enable, Activate, and Sustain

content.text: Champion engagement follows a five-phase lifecycle that spans from early discovery through the sustained rollout phase. Each phase has distinct activities and a distinct relationship between the programme team and the champion.
Phase 1: Identify (Weeks 1–2 of Discovery)

Target: identify 2–3 candidates per pilot team before the use case canvas is finalised. At this stage, candidates are identified but not yet approached. The programme team maintains a candidate list with notes on why each person was identified, who surfaced them, and any early-stage concerns.
Phase 2: Recruit (Weeks 3–4 of Discovery)

A recruitment conversation with each candidate covers the programme goals, the champion role (scope, time commitment, how long), what they will get out of it (early access, visibility, skill development), and what the programme team will do to support them. The conversation must be honest about time commitment — underselling it produces champions who drop out during the pilot when the actual demand exceeds their expectation.

The time commitment for an active pilot champion is approximately 2 hours per week: 30 minutes in the weekly champion sync, 30 minutes answering peer questions and demonstrating the tool, and 60 minutes of their own active use (which produces the ground-level observations that make their feedback valuable). Confirm that the champion's line manager is aware and supportive — a manager who schedules over champion activities or signals that the role is a distraction undermines engagement within weeks.
Phase 3: Enable (Before Pilot Launch)

Champions receive a 3-hour deep-dive session before the pilot launches. This is not the same training the rest of the user base receives — it goes deeper, covers the rationale behind design decisions, and explains what the tool cannot do as well as what it can. A champion who over-promises the tool's capabilities to peers and then is contradicted by the tool's actual performance loses credibility rapidly.
The champion toolkit — a package of supporting materials the champion can use in peer conversations — should include:
A one-page summary of what the Claude integration does and the specific use cases it covers
A list of common questions and honest answers (including 'things it does not do well yet')
A demonstration script for a 5-minute informal show-and-tell
An escalation path for issues they cannot resolve: who to contact, how, within what timeframe
A feedback form or channel for collecting structured input from peers
Phase 4: Activate (During Pilot)

Weekly 30-minute champion syncs during the pilot serve two functions: they give the programme team structured insight into ground-level experience, and they give champions a forum to share what they are seeing and get responses to issues. Champions who attend the sync and raise issues that are addressed quickly become more engaged; champions whose concerns go unacknowledged disengage.

The champion sync agenda: round-table on usage observations (5 min per champion), common questions raised by peers (10 min), issues requiring programme team action (10 min), next week's focus area (5 min). Notes are taken and action owners assigned. Champions who raised issues that were resolved should be explicitly told what happened — closing the loop reinforces that their input matters.
Phase 5: Sustain (Post-Rollout)

After rollout, the champion network transitions from a delivery resource to an ongoing adoption support function. Monthly check-ins (30 minutes, group format) replace weekly syncs. Champions move from supporting the initial rollout to supporting the ongoing evolution of the tool — new use cases, updated prompts, expanded data sources.

Recognition matters more than most programme teams anticipate. Champions are performing a service that has no formal job description and often no formal reward. Mechanisms that work: internal visibility (mention in all-hands meetings, programme newsletter features), access to advanced training or external AI events, priority access to new tool capabilities, and — most effectively — direct appreciation from the executive sponsor. A 5-minute conversation between the BU Director and a champion who has been particularly active produces more sustained motivation than a formal award.
Champion Network Size and Structure
A champion network that is too small cannot cover the user population. One that is too large creates coordination overhead and dilutes the per-champion value the programme team can provide.
Deployment Size
Recommended Champion Ratio
Network Size
Coordination Format
Pilot: 10–20 users
1–2 champions
Informal; direct programme team contact
Small rollout: 20–50 users
2–5 champions
Weekly sync call; shared Slack channel
Medium rollout: 50–150 users
4–10 champions
Weekly sync + monthly in-person; champion lead appointed
Large rollout: 150+ users
8–20 champions
Tiered structure: regional leads + site champions; monthly all-champion call

For large deployments, appoint a champion lead — typically the most engaged and effective champion from the pilot phase. The champion lead takes on coordination responsibility (running the sync, maintaining the champion Slack channel, onboarding new champions as rollout expands) and is the primary point of contact between the champion network and the programme team. This reduces programme team overhead and gives the network a self-sustaining structure.
Managing Champion Attrition
Champion attrition — where recruited champions disengage or leave the network before rollout is complete — is the most common failure mode in champion programmes. Causes and mitigations:
Attrition Cause
Early Warning Signal
Mitigation
Time overcommitment
Champion stops attending syncs; feedback submissions stop
Reduce commitment to minimum viable level; share load with second champion in their team
Frustration with unresolved issues
Champion raises the same issue in multiple syncs with no resolution
Establish service level for champion issue resolution (maximum 1 week); escalate unresolved blockers
Role change or departure
Champion announces job change or organisational restructuring
Identify and recruit a successor within 2 weeks; offboard champion with a transition briefing
Tool not meeting expectations
Champion stops using the tool in their own work
Conduct a direct one-on-one to understand the gap; address or reframe scope
Lack of visible impact
Champion expresses doubt about whether their feedback changes anything
Systematically close feedback loops; report changes made as a result of champion input

A champion who has gone quiet is not the same as a champion who is no longer needed. Proactively check in with inactive champions rather than assuming disengagement is acceptable. A champion network that loses three of its five members before rollout is complete has lost most of its value.
What Champions Are Not
Misuse of the champion role creates problems that are worse than having no champion network. Champions are not:
First-line support agents:

Champions answer peer questions informally, but they are not a substitute for a proper support channel. A champion who receives escalating volumes of support requests will burn out. Define the boundary: questions about basic use go to the champion; technical errors, access issues, and data problems go to the programme team support channel.
Programme team enforcers:

A champion who is perceived as pressure-testing colleagues' usage or reporting non-adopters to management loses peer credibility immediately. Champions succeed through voluntary peer authority — the moment they are associated with management surveillance, their influence disappears.
Permanent volunteers:

The champion role has a defined lifecycle: discovery, pilot, rollout, and early steady-state. After the tool is established and usage is normalised, the intensive champion support role becomes redundant. Transitioning champions to a lighter-touch alumni role — available for questions but not actively supporting adoption — respects their contribution without demanding indefinite commitment.
Substitutes for good change management:

A champion network compensates for the gap between formal programme communications and ground-level adoption. It does not substitute for training, clear communication of the 'why', visible executive sponsorship, or a usable product. Champions cannot rescue a tool that does not work well or a deployment that was not communicated honestly.
Measuring Champion Effectiveness

# Champion effectiveness metrics
# Tracked per champion at each weekly sync during pilot

champion_metrics = {
    "champion_id": "ch-003",
    "name": "Priya Nair",
    "team": "Contract Management",
    "weeks_active": 4,

    # Activity metrics (collected weekly)
    "sessions_attended": 4,   # out of 4 weekly syncs
    "peer_questions_handled": 17,
    "demo_sessions_run": 3,
    "feedback_items_submitted": 11,

    # Impact metrics (collected from team usage data)
    "team_active_users_before_champion": 3,   # of 10
    "team_active_users_after_4_weeks": 8,     # of 10
    "adoption_rate_change": 0.50,             # +50 percentage points

    # Sentiment (assessed by programme team at each sync)
    "current_engagement_score": 4,  # 1-5
    "trend": "stable",
}

def champion_health_summary(m):
    attendance = m["sessions_attended"] / m["weeks_active"]
    adoption_delta = m["team_active_users_after_4_weeks"] - m["team_active_users_before_champion"]
    return {
        "champion": m["name"],
        "attendance_rate": f"{attendance:.0%}",
        "adoption_lift": f"+{adoption_delta} active users",
        "engagement": m["current_engagement_score"],
        "status": "healthy" if m["current_engagement_score"] >= 3 and attendance >= 0.75 else "at-risk",
    }

summary = champion_health_summary(champion_metrics)
for k, v in summary.items():
    print(f"{k}: {v}")

# champion: Priya Nair
# attendance_rate: 100%
# adoption_lift: +5 active users
# engagement: 4
# status: healthy

Adoption lift — the change in active usage rate in the champion's team relative to the rest of the user population — is the most direct measure of champion impact. If teams with active champions are achieving 80% adoption while teams without are at 40%, the champion programme is working. If there is no difference, the network is not translating into behaviour change and the programme team should investigate why.
Building the Champion Business Case

Establishing a champion network requires investment: time from the programme team to recruit and support champions, time from business units to release staff from normal duties for champion activities, and in some cases a small budget for recognition activities. This investment must be justified — particularly in resource-constrained BUs where every hour of staff time has an opportunity cost.
Quantifying the Expected Benefit

The adoption lift that a champion network generates can be estimated before deployment and measured after. A useful framing: if the alternative to a champion network is relying on email communications and optional training sessions, historical data from digital transformation programmes suggests that passive rollout achieves 30–40% sustained adoption after 90 days. Active champion networks — when well-run — achieve 60–75% adoption over the same period. On a pilot of 50 users with an estimated efficiency saving of £120 per user per month at full adoption:
Without champions (40% adoption):
20 active users × £120 = £2,400/month in realised savings
With champions (70% adoption):
35 active users × £120 = £4,200/month in realised savings
Monthly uplift from champion programme:
£1,800/month
Annual uplift:
£21,600 — against a champion programme cost of approximately £3,000–£5,000 (staff time) for a 3-month pilot

These numbers are illustrative and depend on the specific use case, the baseline adoption expected without champions, and the realistic adoption ceiling. The point is not to use this specific model, but to structure the business case with real estimates rather than qualitative assertions about the value of change management.
Securing Line Manager Buy-In

The champion programme fails if line managers do not protect champion time. A champion whose manager schedules over their champion activities or signals that it is low priority will deprioritise it within two weeks. Line manager buy-in must be secured explicitly — not assumed from the fact that the BU sponsor endorsed the programme overall.
A one-page briefing for line managers of champion candidates should cover:
What the champion role involves (2h/week, for approximately 10–12 weeks through pilot and early rollout)
What the line manager is being asked to approve (protected time; attendance at weekly sync; early tool access for their team via the champion)
What the BU benefits from having their team member play this role (first adoption advantage; input into tool design; team receives better support than non-champion teams)
The explicit ask: a written confirmation that they support their team member's participation and will protect the required time
Champion Communications and Tools

Champions operate more effectively when they have access to information and resources that the general user population does not. Exclusive early information signals that the programme team values the champion role — and it equips champions to answer questions before colleagues have even thought to ask them.
Champion Communication Channel
Create a dedicated champion communication channel — a Slack channel, a Teams group, or an email distribution list — that delivers:
Weekly updates on programme progress, upcoming changes, and known issues, shared 24–48 hours before the information is released to the wider user base
Weekly sync call notes and action tracking
System change notifications (new features, prompt updates, data source additions) with champion-specific guidance on how to communicate the change to peers
Anonymous questions from peers, submitted via the champion, with programme team answers

The 24–48 hour advance notice is intentional. A champion who learns about a system update at the same time as the rest of the organisation cannot support peers effectively. Advance information gives champions time to understand the change and prepare answers before peers start asking questions.
The Champion Toolkit Contents

The champion toolkit is a practical package of materials, not a library of documentation. Effective toolkits are short, specific to the deployed use case, and updated when the system changes. A toolkit that covers a contract review Claude integration might include:
Toolkit Item
Format
Purpose
Length
What it does + what it doesn't
1-page summary
Quick reference for peer conversations; sets accurate expectations
1 page
5-minute demo script
Script with screenshots
Walk-through for informal team demonstrations
1 page script + 3 screenshots
Common questions and honest answers
FAQ format
Answers to the 10 most-asked questions, including difficult ones about data privacy and job impact
2 pages
Known limitations and workarounds
Bullet list
What the tool does not handle well and how to work around it; reduces user frustration
Half page
Support escalation guide
Simple flowchart
What the champion handles vs. what goes to the programme support channel
Half page
Feedback collection template
Simple form
Structured format for champions to collect and report peer feedback
1 page
Difficult Champion Conversations
Champions encounter conversations that their toolkit does not prepare them for. The programme team should brief champions on how to handle the most sensitive recurring topics:
'Will this replace my job?'

This question requires an honest answer, not reassurance. Champions who say 'no, this won't affect anyone's job' and are later contradicted by reality destroy their own credibility and damage trust in the programme. The honest framing: 'This tool handles the parts of the task that are most time-consuming and repetitive, so you can focus on the parts that require judgement and relationships. What happens to roles longer-term is a question for leadership to answer honestly — and if you have concerns, the right place to raise them is with your line manager.'

Champions should not be put in the position of representing the programme's decisions on workforce planning. If asked directly, the champion should direct the person to the formal programme communications or to their manager.
'The output is wrong / I don't trust it'

Accuracy concerns require a diagnostic response, not a defensive one. Champions should be trained to ask: 'Can you show me the specific output and what you expected instead?' A specific example allows the programme team to investigate and respond with evidence. A champion who defends the tool against accuracy complaints without evidence produces the opposite of trust. If the tool is producing genuinely inaccurate outputs, the champion's role is to escalate it, not explain it away.
'This is just extra work on top of what I already do'

This is the most common and most valid complaint, particularly in the first two weeks of use when the cognitive overhead of the new workflow is highest and the efficiency benefit is not yet realised. Champions should validate the experience rather than dismiss it: 'That's a real pattern in the first couple of weeks — the time it saves isn't visible until you've used it enough for the prompting to become second nature. What specifically is taking longer?' The answer identifies whether the friction is temporary learning curve or a genuine workflow design problem that needs fixing.
Champion Programme Governance
A champion programme without explicit governance gradually loses structure and becomes informal. Key governance elements:
Champion Charter
A one-page champion charter sets out the mutual commitments between the programme and the champion:
Programme commits to:
weekly sync attendance, issue resolution within 5 business days, advance notification of changes, honest information even when the news is difficult, and recognition of the champion's contribution
Champion commits to:

attending the weekly sync or sending a deputy, actively using the tool in their own work, collecting and reporting peer feedback, escalating issues rather than tolerating them, and completing the champion toolkit training before pilot launch

The charter is not a legal document — it is a social contract that makes mutual expectations explicit. Review and re-confirm it at the start of each major phase (pilot launch, full rollout, post-rollout transition).
Champion Review Meetings

Monthly programme review meetings should include a champion programme update: current network size and health, adoption metrics by team (with anonymisation if politically sensitive), issues surfaced and resolved, recognition of strong champion contributions, and any network changes (new champions joining, attrition, role transitions). This keeps champion programme governance visible to programme leadership and signals that the programme team takes the network seriously.
Scaling the Champion Model
For organisations deploying Claude across multiple BUs or globally, the champion model must scale without a linear increase in programme team overhead. Three patterns support this:
Champion of champions:

Appoint a small group of senior champions (3–5) who each coordinate a cluster of local champions. The programme team works with the senior champions directly; senior champions work with their local cluster. This halves the direct programme team contact surface while maintaining network quality.
Champion community of practice:

When champion networks across multiple BU deployments operate simultaneously, create a cross-BU community of practice where champions share what is working and what is not. Champions learn faster from peer champions than from programme team briefings.
Champion alumni as trainers:

Champions who completed a successful pilot become the trainers for the next BU deployment. They carry practical credibility that external trainers and programme team members do not have: 'This is how we did it in Operations and here is what we actually learned.' Alumni-led training sessions produce measurably higher engagement than standard training sessions.

The highest-return investment in a large-scale Claude deployment is not the technology — it is the humans who make it feel normal. A champion network is the mechanism through which a programme's investment in user acceptance becomes adoption momentum that sustains itself.
Summary

Change champions are peer-level advocates who operate where formal programme communications and management mandates cannot reach. Effective champions are selected for natural credibility, curiosity, and process knowledge — not appointed by rank. They go through a structured lifecycle: identified in discovery, recruited before the use case canvas is finalised, enabled before pilot launch, activated during the pilot with weekly structured engagement, and sustained post-rollout through a lighter-touch programme.

Champion networks require active management: time commitment must be realistic, issues must be resolved within a defined SLA, feedback loops must be visibly closed, and attrition must be addressed proactively. At the right ratio and with adequate programme team support, a champion network is one of the highest-return investments in an enterprise Claude deployment.
